final_summary:
On August 5, 2015, a catastrophic event occurred at the Gold King Mine in San Juan County, Colorado, releasing approximately 1 million gallons of acidic wastewater into Cement Creek, a tributary of the Animas River. The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), working with heavy equipment, was attempting to secure and consolidate a safe entry point into the mine to pump and treat the contaminated water, thereby reducing metal pollution flowing out of the mine. However, the operation unexpectedly triggered a massive release of mine waste water into the upper portions of Cement Creek around 10:30 am on Wednesday.

The resulting orange-brown plume of toxic wastewater flowed downstream through Cement Creek and into the Animas River, prompting widespread concern among local authorities and residents. The Animas River was subsequently closed to recreational activities such as tubing, rafting, and kayaking due to the potential risks posed by the contaminants. La Plata County Sheriff Sean Smith announced the closure, citing the uncertainty surrounding the types and levels of toxins present in the river, and stating that the decision was made in consultation with the EPA, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment, the San Juan Basin Health Department, and the Southern Ute Indian Tribe.

As the contaminated water entered the Animas River, the city of Durango took measures to protect its water supply. The city halted pumping raw water from the Animas River to prevent potential contamination of the city reservoir, which serves as a vital secondary source of water during the summer months. Residents were advised to conserve water, and the city implemented temporary restrictions on non-essential water uses, including suspending water deliveries to the Hillcrest Golf Club and Fort Lewis College.

Local businesses reported increased demand for bottled water, with stores struggling to keep up with customer purchases. The city's utilities director, Steve Salka, emphasized the importance of conserving water, noting that the city typically consumes up to 9.2 million gallons per day during hot summer periods but can only draw 5.3 million gallons from the Florida River.

The EPA downplayed the potential impacts on aquatic life, pointing to existing water-quality impairments in the Cement Creek watershed and the Animas River. However, Colorado Parks and Wildlife deployed fish cages in the Animas River to monitor the effects of the spill on fish populations. Peter Butler, co-coordinator of the Animas River Stakeholders Group, noted that the contaminated water contained high levels of iron, aluminum, cadmium, zinc, and copper, and possibly other metals such as lead and arsenic.

As the plume continued downstream, it reached the New Mexico border, prompting the state's governor to express concern over the delayed notification of the spill by the EPA. Communities along the Animas River, including Durango and Farmington, took precautions to minimize exposure to the contaminated water, shutting down water-intake pumps and advising residents to avoid contact with the river until the discoloration had passed. The incident highlighted ongoing concerns about the legacy of mining in the region and the need for effective management of abandoned mines to prevent similar disasters.

reference: – Colorado's Animas River isn't always bright orange, but that's how it looks today after the EPA accidentally spilled a million gallons of mine waste into a tributary. Officials in San Juan County say state officials and the EPA were actually trying to access contaminated water at Gold King Mine in southwest Colorado on Wednesday when they "unexpectedly triggered a large release of mine waste water into the upper portions of Cement Creek," report Time and the Durango Herald. The acidic water—described by one witness as "the filthiest yellow mustard water you've ever seen"—contains sediment and metals that could irritate the skin, reports the AP. There's no risk to drinking water, but residents of communities along the river have been urged to cut back on water usage until the plume moves on. Yesterday the plume reached Durango, Colo., which had stopped pumping water out of the river on Wednesday. "It's really, really ugly," says a La Plata County official. "Any kind of recreational activity on the river needs to be suspended." The waste is now making its way to New Mexico, as the Animas River meets up with the San Juan River in that state before the San Juan joins the Colorado River in Utah. Officials have placed cages of fish in the river to identify any ill effects and are testing the water to identify the specific metals released. An expert guesses the water contains high concentrations of iron, aluminum, cadmium, zinc, and copper, based on previous contamination. It may also contain mercury, lead, and arsenic, he says, "but there's no evidence of that at this point." (One Texas town recently experienced a creepy water problem.)

final_summary:
On Saturday, a chaotic and deadly confrontation unfolded in the Silver Lake neighborhood of Los Angeles when 28-year-old Gene Evin Atkins, who had just shot and wounded his 76-year-old grandmother and 17-year-old girlfriend, led police on a high-speed car chase that culminated in a violent shootout outside a Trader Joe's store. Atkins, driving a 2015 Toyota Camry, crashed into a light pole outside the store, slamming on the brakes and causing him to exit the vehicle. Witnesses reported that Atkins then fired multiple shots at the approaching police officers, prompting them to return fire.

In the ensuing chaos, 27-year-old Trader Joe's store assistant manager Melyda Corado, who had been fleeing the store after hearing the car crash, was struck by a stray police bullet while attempting to enter the store. The bullet first hit her arm and then entered her body, ultimately proving fatal. Corado's death was confirmed by Los Angeles Police Department (LAPD) Chief Michel Moore, who stated that the officers involved in the shootout were devastated by the outcome.

According to eyewitness accounts and physical evidence, Atkins had fired multiple rounds from a semiautomatic pistol at the officers, prompting them to return fire in self-defense. The officers fired eight rounds in total, with one of the bullets striking Corado. Atkins, who was shot in the left arm during the exchange, eventually surrendered to authorities after a three-hour standoff, releasing several hostages and allowing others to escape.

Atkins faces numerous charges, including murder, attempted murder, false imprisonment of hostages, and felony evasion, with his bail set at $18.775 million. The incident has sparked a mix of reactions, with some expressing sympathy for the officers involved and others criticizing their decision to return fire in a crowded area. A GoFundMe page has been established to support Corado's funeral expenses, raising nearly $25,000 as of Monday.

Corado's death serves as a poignant reminder of the risks faced by law enforcement officers and the devastating consequences of split-second decisions made in high-pressure situations. In a statement, Trader Joe's expressed its condolences to Corado's family and those affected by the tragedy, describing her as a valued and beloved crew member.

reference: – Los Angeles Police Department Chief Michel Moore had the toughest of jobs Tuesday: to announce that the Trader Joe's employee killed during a shootout in Los Angeles on Saturday lost her life to a bullet fired by police, not the suspect. "I am truly sorry," Moore said about 27-year-old Melyda Corado's death. "As a father, as an individual, it is unimaginable, the pain of the Corado family, and everyone that knew her. And we share that pain today." He said the officers involved will forever wrestle with the choices they made, though he believes "my officers' actions were taken to defend themselves and in direct response to the deadly threat" and therefore were appropriate. An investigation into the officers' actions is ongoing. ABC News reports he also detailed a timeline of the crime, which was allegedly sparked by a feud between a man and his grandmother over his girlfriend staying at the grandmother's home. Moore said Gene Atkins, 28, shot his grandmother several times and ultimately led police on a chase that ended when his vehicle crashed into a pole outside the grocery store. Moore said Atkins fired at cops as he ran toward the entrance; Corado had come to the same entrance upon hearing the crash and was struck in her left arm in the process. The bullet entered her body. "Miss Corado ran back inside the store and collapsed behind the manager’s desk," Moore said, per the Los Angeles Times. CBS Los Angeles reports that, among other charges, Atkins faces one count of murder in connection with Corado's death.

final_summary:
According to Article II of the US Constitution, the executive power is vested in the President of the United States, who holds office for a term of four years. The President is elected alongside the Vice President through the Electoral College system, where each state appoints electors based on its number of Senators and Representatives in Congress. The electors meet in their respective states, cast ballots for two candidates, and submit their lists to the President of the Senate, who opens and counts the votes. The candidate with the most votes becomes President, unless there is a tie, in which case the House of Representatives chooses the winner. 

In the event of a vacancy in the presidency, the Vice President assumes the role, and Congress can pass legislation to address the situation. The President takes an oath to faithfully execute the office and preserve, protect, and defend the Constitution. They serve as Commander-in-Chief of the armed forces, grant pardons and reprieves, and have the power to negotiate treaties and appoint federal officials with Senate approval.

However, the President's authority is not absolute, as they can be impeached and removed from office for high crimes and misdemeanors. This was demonstrated in the case of President Richard Nixon, who faced impeachment charges in 1974 for his role in the Watergate scandal. The House Judiciary Committee passed two articles of impeachment, accusing Nixon of preventing the administration of justice, obstructing the investigation of the Watergate break-in, and engaging in conduct that impaired the due process of law.

Fast-forward to the present day, President Donald Trump's lawyer, John Dowd, has argued that the President cannot be found guilty of obstruction of justice due to the Constitution's Article II, which grants the President the power to require opinions from executive department heads. However, this argument has been met with skepticism, particularly given Trump's recent tweet suggesting that he knew his former National Security Adviser, Michael Flynn, lied to the FBI, and his subsequent request to then-FBI Director James Comey to let the Flynn case go.

This raises questions about the limits of Presidential power and the extent to which the President can be held accountable for their actions. While the Constitution grants significant authority to the President, it also establishes checks and balances to prevent abuse of power. As the controversy surrounding Trump's presidency continues to unfold, it remains to be seen how these principles will be applied in practice.

reference: – The Trump attorney who says he penned the "sloppy" tweet that some say indicates a possible obstruction of justice isn't done making headlines. John Dowd spoke to Axios' Mike Allen, scoffing at criticism of the tweet regarding Mike Flynn, which suggested Trump knew the former national security adviser had lied to the FBI before Trump canned James Comey. "The tweet did not admit obstruction," Dowd says. "That is an ignorant and arrogant assertion." Plus, the Constitution has Trump's back, per Dowd. "[The] president cannot obstruct justice because he is the chief law enforcement officer under [the Constitution's Article II] and has every right to express his view of any case," he claims. Newsweek notes it's not clear which section of Article II Dowd refers to, though it speculates on one part involving the president seeking the opinion "of the principal Officer in each of the executive Departments, upon any Subject relating to the Duties of their respective Offices." Allen notes that with this pronouncement, Trump's legal team is "setting the stage" to keep Trump insulated from obstruction of justice or collusion charges in the Russia probe—meaning, Allen speculates, that his lawyers may be nervous charges are coming down the pike. But one of Barack Obama's former counsels notes "it is certainly possible" for Trump to have obstructed justice, and Allen points out Richard Nixon's Articles of Impeachment were jump-started with the assertion that Nixon "has prevented, obstructed, and impeded the administration of justice." But whether Trump can or can't be held responsible for such a transgression may be moot, Allen adds. "The one thing everyone agrees on is that the House of Representatives, with its impeachment power, alone decides what is cause for removal from office," he writes. "For now, at least, the House is run by Republicans."

final_summary:
Charlottesville Police Chief Timothy J. Longo held a news conference on Thursday, urging property owners in the city and surrounding Albemarle County to search for any signs of missing University of Virginia student Hannah Graham, who has been missing for nearly two weeks. Search teams have combed through most of the college town and are now expanding their search into rural areas, including farms and woodlands. Longo appealed to real estate agents and others to check vacant homes and buildings, as well as to report any unusual findings such as tire tracks, clothing, or other potential evidence.

Graham, an 18-year-old sophomore, was last seen on September 13, and her iPhone 5S with a pink case remains unaccounted for. Longo emphasized that the longer the search continues, the more challenging it becomes, but expressed hope that Hannah's parents still hold onto. A $100,000 reward is being offered for information leading to her safe return.

The last person known to have seen Graham is Jesse L. "LJ" Matthew Jr., 32, who has been charged with abduction with intent to sexually assault her. Matthew was taken into custody in Galveston, Texas, after a manhunt spanning over a week. He was arrested on a beach in the Bolivar Peninsula, a remote area accessible only by a single road from the mainland.

Matthew was found camping on the beach, and a deputy initially detained him for refusing to identify himself. However, upon discovering his identity, he was taken into custody without incident. Matthew waived extradition and will be sent back to Charlottesville to face charges. His lawyer, James L. Camblos III, described Matthew as "okay considering the circumstances" after speaking with him by phone from jail.

Matthew's presence in Galveston is unclear, but authorities speculate that he may have traveled over 1,300 miles from Charlottesville. The Galveston County Sheriff's Office searched Matthew's car and surrounding area but found no indication of Graham's whereabouts. Matthew's past is being investigated, including his time at Liberty University and Christopher Newport University, where he played football.

At the upscale Tempo Restaurant in Charlottesville, where Graham was last seen, the owner, Brice Cunningham, stated that Graham did not enter the establishment with Matthew but rather waited outside for him while he purchased drinks. This contradicts earlier reports from police that Graham was seen inside the bar with Matthew. Surveillance footage shows Graham walking with Matthew on the Downtown Mall shortly after 1 a.m., heading towards the restaurant.

In Galveston, locals described the Bolivar Peninsula as a secluded area often visited by those seeking solitude. Sheriff Henry Trochesset noted that the area has historically been a hiding spot for individuals, including the infamous serial killer Dean Corll, whose victims were buried on the beach in the 1970s. Matthew's ability to blend in with the area's transient population made it easier for him to evade detection.

reference: – Jesse Matthew, charged with the abduction of missing UVa student Hannah Graham, was found in Galveston, Texas, Wednesday on a "beach out in the boonies," as one Texan put it. After a tip, police spotted Matthew camped out on a sliver of sand on the remote Bolivar Peninsula—the site of a mass grave used decades ago by serial killer Dean Corll and accessible by a single road, the AP reports. Matthew is one of many to hole up on the spot (among others: famed French pirate Jean Lafitte, rumor has it), where some stay for months. "We get lots of weird folks," says a local. "It seems like a good place to seek refuge. It's kind of remote." More on the case from the Washington Post and the Daily Progress: Matthew initially said he was "George Carr," but police discovered his true identity when they ran his car's plates. Authorities have searched the car, beach, and sand dunes. The case has brought to light another accusation against Matthew. An official has confirmed he was accused of raping a woman at Liberty University in 2002, but she didn't proceed with the case. He was a student there at the time. "Basically, the woman was saying she hadn't consented and Matthew was saying she had," says an attorney with the Lynchburg Commonwealth Attorney's Office. Searchers have scoured much of Charlottesville, Va., and are moving on to surrounding farms and woodland. They've asked area real estate agents to inspect any vacant buildings or properties they represent, and have told the public to be on the lookout for Graham's iPhone 5S with a pink case or unexplained tire tracks. Authorities are also looking to identify some of Matthew's favorite fishing spots. Matthew has declined to fight his extradition, and Virginia police have been sent to retrieve him. He should arrive back in Charlottesville in a few days.

final_summary:
Maia Weinstock, the deputy editor of MIT News, has been creating miniature LEGO figurines to honor and promote scientists and engineers since 2009. Her latest project, "Women of NASA," has been selected by LEGO to become an official set, celebrating the contributions of five pioneering women who made historic impacts on the US space program. The set features Margaret Hamilton, a computer scientist who developed software for the Apollo missions; Mae Jemison, the first African-American woman in space; Katherine Johnson, who calculated and verified trajectories for the Mercury and Apollo programs; Sally Ride, the first American woman in space; and astronomer Nancy Grace Roman, a key figure in the planning of the Hubble Space Telescope.

Weinstock's inspiration for the set came from her love of space and NASA, as well as her desire to increase awareness of the significant contributions women have made to space exploration. She hopes the set will encourage young girls to pursue careers in science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) fields. The set was chosen after receiving 10,000 votes in just 15 days through LEGO's Ideas platform, where users can submit proposals for new sets.

The selection process involved an official review by LEGO, following a public voting period. Weinstock aimed to depict a diverse group of women in terms of their fields, cultural backgrounds, and ages, ensuring representation across various areas of NASA's work. The set includes a range of women, from those working in space flight to astronomers like Roman.

Weinstock's passion for promoting women in STEM began with her creation of a LEGO likeness for her friend Carolyn Porco, a planetary scientist. After sharing the image on Twitter, she received positive feedback and continued to create figures for other scientists, including Jane Goodall, Sylvia Earle, Stephen Hawking, and several MIT faculty members.

The "Women of NASA" set aims to raise awareness of the historical contributions of women in STEM fields and provide a role model for young girls. Weinstock believes that making these women visible and accessible through LEGO figures can inspire future generations to pursue careers in science and engineering. As she notes, "It's really important to set an example for girls, as well as for boys, to normalize and make plain that women are expected to be in these fields and that it's not strange or unusual."

reference: – Mathematician Katherine Johnson was one of the women of NASA depicted in the Oscar-nominated film Hidden Figures, and now she's about to get her own Lego figure. Per MIT News, Johnson and four other women involved with the space agency over the years—Margaret Hamilton (computer scientist), Mae Jemison (first African-American woman in space in 1992), Nancy Grace Roman (pioneering NASA female exec and astronomer), and Sally Ride (the first US woman in space in 1983)—are set to be part of Maia Weinstock's "Women of NASA" Lego prototype, which was chosen from the Lego ideas submission website to be made into an actual set. Concepts for potential sets can be posted on the site, and after the public votes (Weinstock, the deputy editor of MIT News, got 10,000 votes for her idea), Lego then reviews eligible projects. Weinstock says she not only wanted to give a nod to the history of women in STEM, but also to "set an example" for both girls and boys that it isn't "strange or unusual" for there to be females working as astronauts, computer programmers, or any other science or technology expert. Lego says more details on Weinstock's set, including how much it will cost and its release date, should be out later this year or in early 2018. (Cambridge is offering a Lego professorship.)

In the small towns surrounding Parsons, Tennessee, a chilling pattern of suspicious incidents has emerged, sparking concerns about a potential link between two separate cases. Heather Sullivan, a 31-year-old resident of Centerville, approximately 45 minutes from Parsons, reported being approached by a tall, thin man outside her home at around 5:30 am in January. The individual allegedly grabbed her arm as she exited her vehicle, causing her to drop a lamp, which shattered upon impact. Her boyfriend intervened, chasing the suspect away.

The incident bears striking similarities to the disappearance of 20-year-old nursing student Holly Bobo, who vanished from her home in Parsons on April 13th. According to her brother, Clint Bobo, Holly was abducted by a man wearing camouflage clothing at approximately 7:30 am. Both incidents occurred near wooded areas, fueling speculation about a possible connection between the two cases.

Tennessee Bureau of Investigation Director Mark Gwyn acknowledged the possibility of a link, stating that his team is exploring all avenues of investigation. In response to the disappearance, a substantial reward of $80,000 has been offered, with $50,000 contributed by the state. Local residents have joined forces to search the woods surrounding Holly's home, where a blood sample was discovered; however, the test results remain undisclosed.

As the search for Holly continues, her family remains hopeful that she may still be alive. In a heart-wrenching appeal, her mother, Karen Bobo, pleaded with the public, saying, "Holly, I love you. Please help her come home to us." With the investigation ongoing, authorities are working tirelessly to unravel the mystery behind Holly's disappearance and determine whether it is linked to Heather Sullivan's harrowing encounter.

Both incidents occurred near wooded areas, fueling speculation about a possible connection between the two cases.

Tennessee Bureau of Investigation Director Mark Gwyn acknowledged the possibility of a link, stating that his team is exploring all avenues of investigation. In response to the disappearance, a substantial reward of $80,000 has been offered, with $50,000 contributed by the state. Local residents have joined forces to search the woods surrounding Holly's home, where a blood sample was discovered; however, the test results remain undisclosed.

As the search for Holly continues, her family remains hopeful that she may still be alive. In a heart-wrenching appeal, her mother, Karen Bobo, pleaded with the public, saying, "Holly, I love you. Please help her come home to us." With the investigation ongoing, authorities are working tirelessly to unravel the mystery behind Holly's disappearance and determine whether it is linked to Heather Sullivan's harrowing encounter.

A "tall, skinny man" accosted her outside her home around 5:30am, grabbing her arm. But she dropped a lamp and her boyfriend, hearing the crash, came out of the house and scared the man away. Katherine Heigl, known for her roles in romantic comedies, has returned to the entertainment industry after a brief hiatus to focus on her personal life. She cited feeling overwhelmed by the demands of her previous work and needing to reassess her career goals. Heigl expressed frustration with the Hollywood machine, stating that it "turned on" her and left her feeling betrayed. 

In retrospect, Heigl acknowledged overcommitting herself to romantic comedies, which she loved but felt became repetitive and stifled her creativity. This realization prompted her to step away from acting to recharge and explore new opportunities. Her decision allowed her to rediscover her passion for acting and find a healthier balance between her professional and personal life.

Heigl's return to the industry includes starring in the TV series "State of Affairs," where she negotiated a unique arrangement with the producers to limit her episode count per season, allowing her to maintain a steady work-life balance. This newfound stability enables her to pursue projects that inspire her while also spending quality time in Utah.

Meanwhile, a different Hollywood romance unfolded between Ryan Gosling and Rachel McAdams during the filming of their 2004 drama "The Notebook." According to director Nick Cassavetes, tensions ran high between the leads, with Gosling reportedly wanting McAdams removed from the set and replaced with another actress due to their lack of chemistry. However, the situation escalated when the two actors engaged in a heated argument, which ultimately led to a temporary improvement in their working relationship.

Gosling reflected on the tumultuous experience in various interviews, describing the dynamic between him and McAdams as "inspiring the worst in each other." Despite their initial animosity, the pair eventually developed feelings for each other and began dating, although their relationship ended in 2007. Gosling humorously described their breakup as a "draw," implying a fierce and intense confrontation.

final_summary:
A devastating report by the Syrian Centre for Policy Research (SCPR) paints a dire picture of the catastrophic impact of nearly five years of conflict in Syria, estimating that 470,000 fatalities have occurred, more than double the previously cited figure of 250,000 by the United Nations. This staggering number represents 11.5% of the country's population, with 400,000 deaths attributed directly to violence and 70,000 resulting from indirect causes such as inadequate healthcare, food shortages, and lack of basic necessities. The report also highlights the alarming decline in life expectancy, dropping from 70 in 2010 to 55.4 in 2015, and a significant rise in poverty, increasing by 85% in 2015 alone.

The SCPR's findings are based on rigorous research methods and extensive fieldwork conducted across Syria, providing a stark contrast to the UN's discontinued count of conflict-related deaths in mid-2014 due to limited access and diminished confidence in data sources. The report emphasizes that the true scale of the crisis has been grossly underestimated, with many non-governmental organizations and the UN ignoring indirect deaths, which are expected to continue rising.

Syria's national wealth, infrastructure, and institutions have been severely eroded, with the country's economy described as a "black hole" draining domestic and external resources. Oil production remains a vital revenue stream for extremist groups, including ISIS, while consumer prices have skyrocketed by 53%, disproportionately affecting those living in conflict zones and besieged areas. The displacement of 45% of the population, approximately 6.36 million internally and over 4 million abroad, has led to a significant contraction of the workforce, with employment conditions and wages deteriorating, particularly for women.

The situation in Aleppo, Syria's largest city, is particularly dire, with 50,000 people fleeing the latest surge in fighting, necessitating urgent aid deliveries of food and water. The city is at risk of being severed from the rest of the country due to a government advance backed by Russian airstrikes and Iranian militiamen. The Syrian opposition is urging immediate action to alleviate the suffering of tens of thousands of civilians, while the US and Russia engage in delicate negotiations in Munich, with the US pushing for an immediate ceasefire and humanitarian access to besieged areas.

Russia has proposed a ceasefire starting on March 1, but the US suspects this may be a ruse to crush moderate rebel groups. The US has countered with a proposal for an immediate ceasefire accompanied by full humanitarian access, which they believe is essential to prevent further irreparable harm to opposition forces. The conflicting interests and priorities of various stakeholders, including the US, Russia, Turkey, and Saudi Arabia, have hindered progress towards a resolution, leaving the fate of Syria hanging in the balance.

reference: – Russia has proposed a March 1 ceasefire in Syria, US officials say, but Washington believes Moscow is giving itself and the Syrian government three weeks to try to crush moderate rebel groups. The United States has countered with demands for the fighting to stop immediately, officials say. The talk of new ceasefire plans comes as the US, Russia and more than a dozen other countries meet in Munich to try to halt five years of civil war in the Arab country. The Guardian reports that according to a new study from the Syrian Center for Policy Research, the war has killed or injured 470,000 people—11.5% of the population—and has also lowered life expectancy from 70 to 55 and almost "completely obliterated" the country's infrastructure. The most recent Russian-backed offensive, near Aleppo, prompted opposition groups to walk out of peace talks last month in Geneva, while forcing tens of thousands of civilians to flee toward the Turkish border. Sources tell the AP that the US can't accept Russia's offer of a March 1 ceasefire because moderate opposition forces could suffer irreversible losses in northern and southern Syria before it ever takes hold. Defense Secretary Ash Carter, meanwhile, says NATO military authorities have been ordered to draw up plans for how the alliance could help shut down illegal migration and people smuggling across the Aegean Sea. Three NATO allies—Turkey, Germany, and Greece—requested alliance participation in an international effort to help end Europe's gravest migration crisis since World War II.

final_summary:
A devastating chemical spill occurred in West Virginia's Elk River, contaminating the water supply for over 300,000 residents and forcing a do-not-use ban on tap water. The spill originated from a 40,000-gallon tank at a Freedom Industries facility along the Elk River, releasing 4-methylcyclohexane methanol (MCHM), a chemical used in coal processing. The leak was not reported to the state's spill hotline until 12:05 pm ET on Thursday, three hours after initial complaints of a licorice-like odor in the tap water were received.

The state Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) believes approximately 7,500 gallons of the chemical leaked, with some contained before entering the river. As a result, 169 people have sought medical attention for symptoms such as nausea, with 10 admitted to hospital but none with life-threatening conditions. The US Chemical Safety Board has launched an investigation into the spill.

Freedom Industries, the owner of the facility, is exempt from regular DEP inspections and permitting due to its status as a chemical storage facility rather than a production facility. However, the DEP has ordered the company to relocate its coal processing chemicals to a safer site and develop a plan to clean up the spill and any soil contamination within 24 hours.

The spill has raised concerns about the state's regulatory framework and its ability to protect the environment and public health. Critics argue that West Virginia's reliance on the coal and chemical industries has led to a lack of effective oversight and enforcement. The state's DEP has faced criticism for its handling of previous incidents, including a 2008 explosion at a Bayer CropScience plant that killed two workers and a 2012 investigation into Massey Energy's Upper Big Branch mine disaster that resulted in 29 fatalities.

Governor Earl Ray Tomblin has announced plans to work with his environmental agency chief to tighten regulations on chemical storage facilities during the upcoming legislative session. Meanwhile, residents continue to rely on bottled water, with many businesses and schools forced to close or operate at reduced capacity due to the contamination. The state's tourism industry is also suffering, with many visitors leaving the area and local businesses struggling to cope with the economic impact of the spill.

As the situation unfolds, officials are cautiously optimistic about the progress made in cleaning up the spill, with the latest tests showing improved water quality. However, the ban on tap water remains in place until further notice, and residents are advised not to drink, bathe, or cook with the potentially contaminated water. The incident serves as a stark reminder of the risks associated with the state's heavy reliance on the coal and chemical industries and highlights the need for stronger regulations and greater accountability to ensure public safety and environmental protection.

"We can’t just point a single finger at this company," says the director of the West Virginia Rivers Coalition, noting that the site of the spill hadn't been inspected since 1991. "We need to look at our entire system and give some serious thought to making some serious reform and valuing our natural resources over industry interests." A tap water ban is still in place in the Charleston region, where many schools and businesses remain closed, but authorities say they can "see the light at the end of the tunnel" and they expect the ban to be lifted within days, starting in downtown Charleston and other priority zones, NBC reports.

Lauren Mann, a 25-year-old woman from Colorado, was found dead in her Vienna apartment on January 26, 2016. Mann had been working as an au pair for a local family and was reported missing by her employer after she failed to show up for work. Police and firefighters forced open her door and discovered her half-naked body on a mattress in the bedroom. 

The initial autopsy did not reveal the cause of death, leading investigators to await the results of toxicological tests, which were expected to take several days to a week to complete. However, subsequent autopsies later confirmed that Mann died from suffocation. Despite the determination of suffocation as the cause of death, police continued to treat the case as a homicide due to the possibility of third-party involvement.

Mann had been living in Vienna for several years, working as an au pair while pursuing further education. She had graduated from the University of Colorado-Boulder's College of Music in 2012. Her employer described her as "very reliable, trusted, caring, and socially active," and her neighbors characterized her as a friendly and outgoing person.

Police have spoken to Mann's friends and neighbors as part of their investigation, but no specific suspect has been identified. The U.S. Embassy in Vienna is providing consular assistance to Mann's family. Mann's hometown in Colorado expressed shock and sadness over her death, with a local resident recalling her as a happy and popular young girl who took dance lessons with her daughter.

ABC News reports Lauren Mann, 25, died by suffocation, though it notes that Vienna police rep Thomas Keiblinger earlier referenced a preliminary autopsy that found no indications of strangulation. NBC News echoes the report of suffocation, but CBS News and the AP report the autopsy was inconclusive, and their reports don't mention suffocation. CBS cites officials who on Thursday confirmed Mann was not strangled, shot, or stabbed. The case is being treated as a homicide, and an official tells NBC News "third party involvement cannot be excluded"; police say there are no suspects at this time. Keiblinger says it'll take a week or two for the toxicology report to be completed, which has CBS saying her death "could be [a] mystery for weeks." ABC reports Mann failed to pick up one of her charges from school on Monday, but her half-dressed body wasn't found until 6pm the following day. The Colorado native was a graduate of the University of Colorado-Boulder's College of Music.

As the US military prepares to withdraw from Iraq by the end of 2011, the Obama administration plans a significant shift towards a civilian-led effort to maintain stability in the country. The State Department will take over the training of the Iraqi police, a task that will primarily be executed by contractors. Two new $100 million outposts will be established to address potential conflicts between the Iraqi Army and Kurdish peshmerga forces in northern Iraq, with American diplomats playing a crucial role in mediating these disputes.

To safeguard civilians vulnerable to Al-Qaeda linked insurgents and Iranian-backed militias, the State Department intends to nearly double its private security personnel in Iraq, increasing the total to approximately 7,000. These contractors will be stationed at five fortified compounds across the country, operating radar systems to detect incoming rocket attacks, searching for roadside bombs, flying reconnaissance drones, and providing rapid response teams to assist civilians in distress.

This unprecedented civilian-led operation marks a significant departure from previous military-led efforts in Iraq. According to James Dobbins, a former ambassador with extensive experience in conflict zones, this endeavor is "unprecedented in scale." Despite concerns raised by some veterans of the Iraq War, including Ambassador Ryan C. Crocker, who suggests that thousands of additional troops may be necessary to ensure stability, the Obama administration remains committed to its plan.

In preparation for the withdrawal, the US military is working to reduce troop levels to 50,000 by August 31st, meeting President Obama's deadline for the end of combat operations. However, the majority of these troops will remain in Iraq until the end of 2011 to continue training Iraqi forces. Following the withdrawal, the mission in Iraq will be renamed Operation New Dawn, with a focus on providing support and economic development assistance to the country.

The US will also maintain a significant presence through private security contractors, who will play a critical role in protecting civilians and addressing potential security threats. As the last of the military troops depart, the Obama administration emphasizes its continued commitment to Iraq, with Secretary of State Hillary Clinton stating that the US will "continue to do our part to support the brave men and women in uniform that have sacrificed so much."

President Obama hailed the withdrawal as a milestone in the Iraq War, acknowledging the sacrifices made by US troops and civilians. He emphasized that while the combat mission is ending, the US will continue to build a strong partnership with the Iraqi people through increased diplomatic efforts and a civilian commitment.

final_summary:
Alan Miller McCarty, a 36-year-old man from Milton in Santa Rosa County, Florida, was found guilty on all charges related to his threats to kill Circuit Judge Stasia Warren, who had previously ruled in a custody case involving McCarty. During his trial, McCarty displayed erratic behavior, yelling at, interrupting, and crudely insulting the presiding judge, Circuit Judge Matt Foxman, and others in the courtroom. Despite being moved to a separate room with one-way glass, McCarty continued to scream expletives and insults, including making a derogatory comment towards Judge Foxman, which the judge politely declined to respond to.

The jury, consisting of three women and three men, deliberated for approximately 30 minutes before finding McCarty guilty of two counts of threats or extortion and two counts of corruption by threat against a public official. These charges stemmed from phone calls McCarty made to dispatchers and Judge Warren, in which he threatened to kill her and described graphic violence. 

McCarty claimed that he made these threats because he was upset over the custody case and felt that Judge Warren was unfairly taking his children away from him. His children had been living in Texas since 2007, and McCarty had not seen them for two years prior to his arrest in September 2019. 

During the trial, McCarty's defense attorney, Ryan Belanger, argued that his client was a "very squeaky wheel" who felt he had no other option but to make the threats due to his desperation. However, prosecutors countered that McCarty's words carried weight and that Judge Warren was indeed fearful of his threats.

In addition to the original charges, McCarty faced further accusations of threatening employees at Stewart-Marchman-Act Behavioral Healthcare. Following his conviction, McCarty was sentenced to 20 years in prison, with State Attorney R.J. Larizza stating that this sentence would provide ample time for McCarty to reflect on the gravity of his actions against the judicial system.

On May 6, 2016, a series of violent incidents unfolded in Montgomery County, Maryland, resulting in the deaths of three individuals and injuring three others. The chain of events began on Thursday evening when Eulalio Tordil, a 62-year-old federal law enforcement officer employed by the Federal Protective Service, shot and killed his estranged wife, Gladys Tordil, outside High Point High School in Beltsville, Prince George's County. The shooting occurred as Gladys Tordil was picking up her children from school, and a bystander who attempted to intervene was also shot and sustained non-life-threatening injuries.

Tordil had a history of domestic abuse, as revealed in a protective order issued against him in March 2016 by a Prince George's County district judge. The order prohibited Tordil from contacting his estranged wife and two minor children, and required him to surrender his firearms, including three revolvers, except for use during work hours. Despite this, Tordil continued to pose a threat to his family and others.

A recent wave of misinformation has swept through social media platforms, particularly Twitter and Reddit, claiming that the latest iOS 8 update allows for wireless charging via microwave ovens. However, this claim has been thoroughly debunked as a hoax originating from a 4chan thread. According to reports, users who fell victim to the false information attempted to charge their iPhones in the microwave, resulting in damaged devices and potentially hazardous situations.

This is not the first instance of such a hoax, as videos on YouTube have circulated for years promoting the same false feature. In fact, 4chan has a history of perpetuating similar pranks, including a previous attempt to deceive Apple users into believing that iOS 7 would render their phones waterproof.

The "Wave" hoax gained traction despite being easily verifiable as false, with some individuals even sharing photos of damaged iPhones and microwaves online. Fortunately, no serious injuries or fatalities have been reported. Users are reminded that they can opt out of storing location data on Twitter and delete their location history at any time.

The hoax is billed as "Wave," reports Mashable, an apparent "iOS8 exclusive" in which you purportedly charge your phone "wirelessly through microwave frequencies ... using any standard household microwave." The slick-looking ad for Wave, which has been making the rounds on Twitter and Reddit, helpfully recommends "60 seconds at 700W or 70 seconds at 800W." It's completely unknown how many suckers fell for Wave, but Mashable notes that "photos of fried iPhones and burned microwaves" are circulating. And apparently enough of them that the LAPD got in on the action, reports the LA Times. "This #Wave capability is a #hoax. Don't be fooled into microwaving your #iPhone6. #Apple #Smartphone," they tweeted. Meanwhile, angry Apple users with crispy iPhones aren't the only ones torqued at 4Chan: Celebrities are apparently firing back, too.

In a shocking turn of events, Iraqi military forces, trained and equipped by the US and coalition forces, have surrendered without a fight to insurgent groups in several cities, including Mosul and Tikrit. This development raises questions about what went wrong in the training and equipping process. According to US officials, the US has been secretly flying unmanned surveillance aircraft over Iraq since last year to gather intelligence on insurgents, but the program has proven ineffective in preventing the swift takeover of key cities.

The fall of Mosul and Tikrit marks a significant milestone for the Islamic State in Iraq and the Levant (ISIS), a Sunni Islamist group estimated to have between 3,000 to 5,000 fighters. The group has exploited the deep-seated grievances of the Sunni Arab community, which feels marginalized by the Shia-dominated government of Prime Minister Nouri Maliki. ISIS has already taken control of Ramadi and Falluja, but the capture of Mosul, a city of over 1 million people, represents its greatest achievement yet.

President Barack Obama has stated that the US is considering all options, including military action, to aid Iraq in its fight against ISIS, but has ruled out deploying ground troops. The White House has confirmed that US defense contractors working with the Iraqi military are being relocated to safer areas due to security concerns. The UN Security Council has expressed its support for the Iraqi government and people in their fight against terrorism, while warning of a dire humanitarian situation unfolding in Mosul, where up to 500,000 people have fled.

As the situation continues to deteriorate, fears are growing of a wider Sunni uprising and potential regional instability. Former Baath Party members loyal to Saddam Hussein have reportedly joined forces with ISIS, raising concerns about the spread of extremism. The Iraqi parliament's inability to pass a vote granting Prime Minister Maliki emergency powers highlights the country's fragmented politics and lack of unity in the face of crisis.

Meanwhile, Kurdish forces have seized control of the oil-rich city of Kirkuk, which has long been a point of contention between the central government and the Kurdistan Regional Government. The Kurds see Kirkuk as a strategic prize and a means to expand their autonomous region. As the situation in Iraq continues to unfold, the international community remains on high alert, with the US, UK, and France weighing their options in response to the rapidly evolving crisis.

In a heart-wrenching appeal, 14-year-old Santiago resident Valentina Maureira, who suffers from cystic fibrosis, has taken to social media to plead with Chilean President Michelle Bachelet to grant her permission to end her life through euthanasia. The desperate teenager, who has undergone five painful surgeries due to her condition, expressed her desire to be allowed to pass away peacefully in a video uploaded to her Facebook page. 

Valentina's family has been deeply affected by the disease, having lost her older brother to cystic fibrosis earlier. Her father, Freddy Maureira, revealed that his daughter has endured immense suffering and pain, despite undergoing multiple operations that were initially promised to improve her condition. However, these treatments only worsened her situation.

The Universidad Catolica clinic in Santiago confirmed that Valentina is indeed a patient there, stating that she is currently stable and not facing any life-threatening conditions. Despite this, the government has ruled out euthanasia as an option, citing the existing laws in Chile that prohibit such practices. According to government spokesman Alvaro Elizalde, the health ministry is working closely with the family to provide Valentina with necessary psychological and medical support.

President Bachelet, who has been at the center of controversy over her proposed reforms, including a bill to relax the country's strict abortion laws, has yet to address the issue of euthanasia directly. The Catholic Church's significant influence in Chile has contributed to the country's stance on euthanasia, making it one of the few nations where the practice remains outlawed.

Following Valentina's emotional plea, President Bachelet paid a visit to the teenager at the hospital, sparking further debate on the topic of euthanasia in Chile. Valentina's case has brought attention to the harsh realities faced by those suffering from cystic fibrosis, a life-threatening disease that causes damage to the lungs and digestive system. Her struggles, including persistent vomiting and debilitating headaches, have left her family and supporters advocating for her right to choose a peaceful end to her suffering.

reference: – Valentina Maureira's brother died from cystic fibrosis when he was 6. The 14-year-old has the same inherited disease and was told she has a life expectancy of 17, reports CNN. Except Valentina doesn't want to live that long. The Chilean teen posted a now-viral video to Facebook late last month, a plea to President Michelle Bachelet that she be allowed to end her life. "I am tired of living with this sickness," which causes damage to the lungs and digestive system, Valentina explains. "Please authorize an injection so I can sleep forever." But a rep for Bachelet on Thursday explained "it's impossible to grant her wish" due to Chilean law, which bars euthanasia and doesn't allow a government official to circumvent that. Still, the president herself, who is also a pediatrician, did visit Valentina for more than an hour last week; the two posed for a selfie. The president's rep explained "we will provide all the emotional and psychological support and medical treatment to improve her living conditions." As for her medical condition, the Universidad Catolica clinic in Santiago describes it as stable and notes she is not facing, as Reuters puts it, any "immediately life-threatening conditions." But her father described a painful existence: "She has already had five operations ... which have caused her a lot of suffering. It was promised that things would get better, but for her it was worse." She suffers from persistent headaches and vomiting as well. In an interview with the AP, he says he "cried through the night" after learning what Valentina wanted, "but I have to respect her decision because she's the one who's suffering this illness." (One country has made euthanasia legal for kids.)

In Oklahoma, a contentious dispute has emerged over the legitimacy of Representative Frank Lucas's recent primary election victory. Challenger Tim Murray, who secured just 5% of the vote, has announced plans to contest the outcome, citing allegations that Lucas is not the genuine article but rather a body double. According to Murray, Lucas was executed by the World Court in Ukraine on January 11, 2011, and has since been replaced by a lookalike. This assertion is based on information posted on Murray's campaign website, where he claims that Lucas's duplicate was put on trial and found guilty in Ukraine. 

Lucas, a 20-year incumbent, has dismissed these claims as baseless and absurd, stating that he is indeed alive and has never traveled to Ukraine. His campaign team has declined to comment further on the matter. Despite the lack of evidence supporting Murray's claims, the challenger remains resolute in his stance, even going so far as to assert that he would never employ a lookalike or artificial intelligence to represent him in office. 

This unusual controversy has drawn attention from local experts, with Keith Gaddie, a University of Oklahoma professor, describing the situation as one of the most bizarre occurrences in Oklahoma politics. Paul Ziriax, head of the Oklahoma State Election Board, notes that Murray has yet to file a formal petition to challenge the election results, with a deadline set for later in the day. As the situation unfolds, it remains to be seen whether Murray's claims will gain traction or be dismissed as the product of a desperate attempt to salvage a losing campaign.

John Stamos, the American actor best known for his roles in the popular sitcoms "Full House" and "Fuller House," is expecting his first child with his fiancée Caitlin McHugh. The 54-year-old actor revealed the news to People magazine, stating that he and McHugh had previously discussed starting a family and decided to take the leap. According to Stamos, the couple's decision to have a child came after McHugh playfully joked that he was "old" and suggested they conceive before getting married.

Stamos, who has been open about wanting to start a family, expressed excitement about becoming a father, saying he "always wanted to be a dad" but wasn't sure if it was possible due to his advanced age. However, with McHugh by his side, he feels confident and ready to take on the new role. The couple got engaged in October 2017 at Disneyland, a special place for McHugh, and Stamos created a romantic video proposal featuring scenes from various Disney movies.

McHugh, 31, also expressed her enthusiasm about becoming a mother, describing Stamos as a man with a "biggest, most loving and generous heart." She praised his qualities such as originality, creativity, ambition, work ethic, and humor, which she believes he will pass down to their child. The couple is keeping the baby's sex a secret for now but plans to continue expanding their family in the future, with both Stamos and McHugh expressing interest in having multiple children.

This news marks another addition to the list of celebrities who have become fathers in their 50s, joining the likes of George Clooney, Alec Baldwin, Steve Martin, Clint Eastwood, Larry King, Robert De Niro, Rod Stewart, Hugh Hefner, and David Letterman, who have all welcomed children at an advanced age.

Tennessee Virtual Academy, a for-profit online public school operated by K12 Inc., has been embroiled in controversy over allegations of manipulating student grades to conceal poor academic performance. An email obtained by NewsChannel 5 Investigates reveals that the school's leadership instructed teachers to delete failing grades from the computer system, replacing them with higher marks to show improved student progress. The email, dated December, directed teachers to remove October and September progress reports, leaving only November grades visible, and to erase failing grades from assignments where most students scored poorly. This practice, described as "cheating" by State Representative Gloria Johnson, a Democrat and career teacher, raises concerns about the integrity of the school's reporting and accountability.

According to the email, teachers were also advised to prioritize reading and math grades, while downplaying the importance of history and science grades. When questioned about the email, K12 officials claimed that the intention behind deleting grades was to "more accurately recognize students' current progress" and to help identify students needing additional support. However, critics argue that this approach masks the true extent of student struggles and undermines the validity of the school's assessment methods.

State Representative Mike Stewart, a Democrat from Nashville, introduced a bill (HB728) aimed at repealing the 2011 law allowing for-profit virtual schools to operate in Tennessee, citing concerns about the financial burden on taxpayers and the poor academic performance of students enrolled in these schools. Stewart pointed out that K12 Inc. received $7.5 million in state funding this year, with each student generating approximately $5,300 in revenue for the company. Despite claims by K12 officials that the company loses money, Stewart estimates that K12 spends between $1,000 to $2,000 per student.

During a House Education Subcommittee meeting, Stewart presented an email sent to Virtual Academy teachers instructing them to delete failing grades, which he argued demonstrated a clear intent to manipulate student records. However, a teacher from the school, Summer Shelton, defended the email, stating that it was intended to clarify the use of a new grade book system, which allows teachers to report student progress using the most recent grade rather than averaging grades. While Shelton acknowledged that some students may have retaken tests, resulting in higher grades being recorded, she maintained that the email was not intended to deceive or mislead.

Despite the controversy surrounding the email, the Haslam administration-backed bill, sponsored by Representative Harry Brooks, was approved by the subcommittee on a 6-3 vote. The revised bill imposes a 1,500-student cap on new virtual schools, but excludes existing schools like Tennessee Virtual Academy, which currently enrolls approximately 3,200 students. The bill also grants the state education commissioner the authority to close a virtual school with consistently poor performance or impose an enrollment cap.

However, a teacher from the school, Summer Shelton, defended the email, stating that it was intended to clarify the use of a new grade book system, which allows teachers to report student progress using the most recent grade rather than averaging grades. While Shelton acknowledged that some students may have retaken tests, resulting in higher grades being recorded, she maintained that the email was not intended to deceive or mislead.

Despite the controversy surrounding the email, the Haslam administration-backed bill, sponsored by Representative Harry Brooks, was approved by the subcommittee on a 6-3 vote. The revised bill imposes a 1,500-student cap on new virtual schools, but excludes existing schools like Tennessee Virtual Academy, which currently enrolls approximately 3,200 students. The bill also grants the state education commissioner the authority to close a virtual school with consistently poor performance or impose an enrollment cap.

reference: – Tennessee Virtual Academy, a for-profit, online public school heavily supported by state Republicans, has found a novel way of boosting student performance—just delete bad grades, reports News Channel 5 in Nashville. A leaked December email from the school's VP appears to tell its middle-school teachers to erase some scores that don't measure up. "If you have given an assignment and most of your students failed that assignment, then you need to take that grade out," it reads. "To me, this appears like it's grade fixing," said one Democratic state representative. Virginia-based parent company K12 is refusing to talk, but the TVA principal said the deletions were intended to "more accurately recognize students' current progress." But as the two-year-old school will receive $7.5 million in funding from the state this year, many Tennessee lawmakers are outraged. The Knoxville News Sentinel reports that a House bill that proposed shutting the school altogether was killed yesterday; a second was passed by the House Education Subcommittee. It would allow the state to shutter a virtual school that records sub-standard student performance two years in a row. (Meanwhile, a Lehigh University student is suing over a bad grade.)

final_summary:
On November 5, 2017, a devastating mass shooting took place at the First Baptist Church in Sutherland Springs, Texas, leaving 26 people dead and 20 others injured. The shooter, identified as Devin Patrick Kelley, 26, opened fire on the congregation during Sunday morning services, targeting a small, tight-knit community where everyone knew each other. Kelley, a former Air Force member, had a violent past, having been court-martialed in 2012 for assaulting his spouse and child, and serving a year in prison before receiving a bad conduct discharge in 2014.

The shooting occurred at approximately 11:20 a.m. local time, with Kelley entering the church wearing all-black tactical gear and a ballistic vest. Witnesses reported hearing gunfire and seeing people running for cover as Kelley continued to shoot. A local resident, Stephen Willeford, who was at the scene, grabbed his rifle and engaged Kelley, chasing him in a truck driven by Johnnie Langendorff. Kelley eventually crashed his vehicle and was found dead, although the exact cause of death remains unclear.

The victims ranged in age from 5 to 72 years old, with eight patients being treated at Connally Memorial Medical Center in nearby Floresville, Texas. Four of these patients were transferred to University Hospital in San Antonio for further treatment, with one requiring airlift due to critical condition. Three patients were released from Connally Memorial Medical Center, while one remained in stable condition.

The First Baptist Church, a hub for community services and outreach programs, offered free home-cooked meals to anyone who walked through the door every Thursday. The church's pastor, Frank Pomeroy, and his wife, Sherri, lost their 14-year-old daughter, Annabelle, in the shooting. Pastor Pomeroy described Annabelle as "one very beautiful, special child."

As the community grappled with the tragedy, residents expressed shock and grief, with many struggling to comprehend why such an event could occur in their peaceful town. "You'd never think something like this would happen out here – it's so small," said Nick Uhlig, a lifelong resident of Sutherland Springs. The town's residents came together to mourn the loss of their loved ones, holding vigils and prayer sessions, and setting up a makeshift memorial with 26 crosses to honor the victims.

President Donald Trump condemned the shooting as an "act of evil" and called it "horrific," while Texas Governor Greg Abbott declared it the deadliest mass shooting in Texas' modern-day history. The incident sparked widespread outrage and calls for stricter gun control measures, with many questioning how Kelley was able to obtain firearms despite his violent past.

As the investigation continues, authorities are working to piece together the events surrounding the shooting and determine the motives behind Kelley's actions. The FBI and local law enforcement agencies are conducting a thorough investigation, collecting evidence and interviewing witnesses to understand the circumstances leading up to the tragedy.

In the aftermath of the 2012 Sandy Hook Elementary School massacre in Newtown, Connecticut, Sandy Hook Promise, a national non-profit organization founded by family members of the victims, was established to protect children from gun violence. The organization aims to honor the memory of those lost by transforming their tragedy into a catalyst for change, providing programs and practices that safeguard children and prevent senseless loss of life. 

To raise awareness about the warning signs of potential shooters, Sandy Hook Promise launched the "Know the Signs" campaign, featuring a powerful public service announcement (PSA) titled "While You Were Watching Evan." The PSA, released in 2018, tells the story of Evan, a high school student who becomes infatuated with a mysterious classmate through secret messages exchanged on a school library desk. As Evan's romantic escapades unfold, the viewer is distracted from the subtle yet ominous presence of another student in the background, displaying concerning behavior indicative of potential violence.

This second student is seen browsing gun-related content on YouTube, being bullied by peers, sharing images of guns on social media, and reading gun enthusiast magazines. Meanwhile, Evan remains oblivious to the danger lurking behind him, only to discover his secret admirer's identity on the final day of school. The ominous figure, armed with an assault rifle, suddenly appears, highlighting the devastating consequences of ignoring warning signs.

Sandy Hook Promise emphasizes that recognizing one warning sign alone does not necessarily indicate a person is planning violence, but rather, multiple connected signs over time may signal a trajectory towards harm. The organization encourages viewers to become aware of these signs and intervene to prevent such tragedies. According to Sandy Hook Promise, 80% of school shooters and 70% of individuals who took their own lives shared their intentions with someone before acting, yet no one intervened.

Through the "Know the Signs" campaign, Sandy Hook Promise aims to educate the public on identifying at-risk behaviors and empowering them to take action, ultimately saving lives.

reference: – Meet Evan. He's a bored high school kid who starts defacing school property, er, doodling on a table in the library one day. Someone writes back. Messages are swapped as days pass, mystery and romance bloom. Evan wanders the halls, eyeing his classmates, wondering who his mystery penpal is, and as AdWeek puts it, "there ensues a back-and-forth that's pretty captivating. Perhaps too captivating." (To avoid spoilers, stop reading and watch the video.) The end result has been described as "jarring" (Kansas City Star), "chilling" (Mic), and "powerful" (People). While you and Evan are busy being captivated—especially as Evan meets his mystery penpal—one of Evan's classmates is lurking in the background providing warning signs that he's planning a shooting, which in fact interrupts that very meeting we've been waiting for. The ad comes courtesy of Sandy Hook Promise, run by family of the victims of the school shooting in Newtown, Conn. "When you don't know what to look for, or can't recognize what you are seeing, it can be easy to miss warning signs or dismiss them as unimportant," says Nicole Hockley, whose 6-year-old son, Dylan, died in the massacre. The organization, which says 80% of shooters tell someone what they're planning, aims to shine a light on the need to watch for those signs. (In Utah, a teen's own parents disarmed him inside a school.)

In Pennsylvania, inheritance tax is levied on the transfer of a deceased person's assets to beneficiaries through their will, heirs by intestacy, or transferees by law. The tax rate varies based on the recipient's relationship to the deceased individual. According to the state's regulations, no inheritance tax is applied to transfers made to a surviving spouse or to a parent from a child under the age of 21. Direct descendants and lineal heirs receive a 4.5% tax rate, while siblings are subject to a 12% rate. Other heirs, excluding charitable organizations, exempt institutions, and government entities exempt from taxation, face a 15% tax rate. Notably, property held jointly by spouses is exempt from this tax. 

A significant change was implemented for estates of individuals who passed away after June 30, 2012, allowing certain farm land and agricultural properties to be exempt from inheritance tax if they are transferred to eligible recipients. This exemption requires compliance with specific conditions outlined in Inheritance Tax Informational Notice 2012-01. 

Pennsylvania inheritance tax payments are due at the time of the decedent's passing but become delinquent nine months later. However, a 5% discount is granted when the payment is made within three months of the decedent's death. For additional guidance and frequently asked questions regarding inheritance tax, residents can refer to the brochure "Pennsylvania Inheritance Tax and Safe Deposit Boxes." Those seeking more detailed and historical information on Pennsylvania inheritance tax should consult the Tax Compendium.

reference: – Roland Bosee Jr., 68, and Nino Esposito, 78, have been inseparable since they first met in 1970. "You meet someone and it just clicks," Esposito told the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette last month. So with the SCOTUS decision over the summer to legalize gay marriage, the men looked forward to finally tying the knot. But one issue is holding them back: Esposito is technically Bosee's father, because he adopted him in 2012. "We thought never in our lifetime—or in 20 lifetimes—...same-sex marriage [would] happen," Bosee said, adding to CNN that the adoption "gave us the most legitimate thing available to us" at the time. Even though they petitioned to have the adoption annulled, Allegheny County Judge Lawrence O'Toole —known for being progressive on LGBT issues—rejected that plea, noting that adoptions are typically only dissolved when fraud is involved and that nixing theirs "would place in jeopardy and imperil adoption decrees generally." Adoptions of this sort are often sought to cement legal status for financial and other reasons, as well as to strengthen emotional ties. The men said it was both: Esposito told the Post-Gazette "we felt we lacked a family of our own," but they also discovered that any money they inherited from each other would be subject to a much greater inheritance tax if they weren't considered relatives. One person on the couple's side: Pennsylvania Sen. Bob Casey, who tells the Post-Gazette, "I wasn't aware that LGBT couples were turning to this method. I can't even imagine having to get an adoption petition approved … to visit a loved one in the hospital." Casey has written a letter of support to AG Loretta Lynch, asking her office to "consider issuing guidance" in cases like this; her office is reviewing it, a rep tells CNN. (The gay marriage decision held up a heterosexual couple's divorce.)

final_summary:
In a significant development for survivors of child sexual abuse in Utah, the state legislature has passed a bill eliminating the statute of limitations for lawsuits against perpetrators of such crimes. The legislation, known as HB277, aims to provide victims with more time to heal and gather the courage to seek justice, rather than being forced to confront their abusers within a limited timeframe.

Deondra Brown, co-founder of the non-profit Foundation for Survivors of Abuse, welcomed the passage of the bill, stating that it allows victims to "breathe, take their time in healing, and come forward and tackle that big legal case when and if they are every ready." Brown's own experiences as a survivor of childhood abuse by her father, Keith Brown, informed her advocacy for the bill. Her sisters, also victims of their father's abuse, pursued criminal charges against him in 2010, resulting in his conviction and sentencing to 10 years to life in prison.

Under current Utah law, civil actions against perpetrators of child abuse are limited to four years after the victim turns 18. However, HB277 removes this limitation, giving victims a four-year window from the time of discovery or remembrance of the abuse to file a civil lawsuit. This change is expected to allow victims to overcome emotional obstacles that often prevent them from seeking justice.

The bill's sponsors, including Rep. Ken Ivory and Sen. Aaron Osmond, emphasized the importance of holding perpetrators accountable for their crimes, regardless of the time elapsed since the abuse occurred. According to Osmond, approximately 1 in 6 boys and 1 in 4 girls in Utah will suffer or have suffered from sexual abuse, and most children do not realize or cope with the trauma until after the age of 40.

Not all lawmakers supported the bill, with some expressing concerns about potential false accusations and the impact on defendants. However, the measure ultimately passed the Senate with a 24-2 vote.

Meanwhile, in a separate case, a former high school teacher, Michael Layne Williamson, was sentenced to prison for sexually abusing a student 22 years ago. The victim, now 35, shared a powerful testimony during the sentencing hearing, detailing the lasting effects of the abuse and the guilt and shame she carried for decades. Williamson apologized to the court, the community, and the victim, acknowledging his actions as "heinous" and pleading for forgiveness.

Judge Thomas Willmore delivered a stern rebuke to Williamson, emphasizing that his actions were not a "mistake" but a deliberate breach of trust and the law. The judge sentenced Williamson to one to 15 years in prison, with an additional term of up to five years to run concurrently, and ordered him to complete a sex offender program while incarcerated. The victim expressed relief and gratitude for the justice served, but also acknowledged the ongoing struggle to protect her own children from similar harm.

reference: – A retired Utah school teacher was sent to prison for having sex with one of his teenage students 22 years ago, reports the Deseret News. The woman came forward last year and told police that former teacher Michael Layne Williamson, 60, had repeatedly assaulted her over a period of years. He was sentenced to one to 15 years in prison by a judge who berated him for disgracing the "noble profession" of teaching. Williamson, who pleaded guilty in May to aggravated sexual abuse of a child and another count of attempted sexual abuse, apologized in court. "This indiscretion of mine was of the most heinous type," he said. "Let me stop you right there," retorted Judge Thomas WIllmore. "It is not an indiscretion. You broke the law." The unidentified victim, now 35, displayed a photo of herself as an eighth-grader and said Williamson ruined her life. "You put it all on my shoulders, those of a 13-year-old, to carry," she said. "I always blamed myself." She said she left the state and kept silent but on a trip back home, the pain was too much. Supporters of Williamson, however, wrote that he was a respected teacher and golf coach who “fell victim to the persuasions of a girl,” reports the Herald Journal. It's possible this isn't the end of Williamson's legal trouble: Last year, the state changed the law to allow victims of sexual abuse to file civil lawsuits years after the fact, notes the Deseret News. (In Texas, this teacher was accused of having a longtime sexual relationship with an eighth-grade student.)

final_summary:
On October 27, 2018, a devastating attack occurred at the Tree of Life synagogue in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, leaving 11 people dead and six injured. The shooting is considered the deadliest attack on the Jewish community in the United States, surpassing the 1985 killing of Charles Goldmark, his wife, and their two sons by David Morse Rice. The Anti-Defamation League (ADL) stated that the attack is "unconscionable" and "unthinkable" given the current state of affairs.

Robert Bowers, a 46-year-old man, has been charged with 29 federal counts, including 11 counts of using a firearm to commit murder and multiple counts of hate crimes. According to the police affidavit, Bowers expressed hatred towards Jews and stated that he wanted all Jews to die, as well as claiming that Jews were committing genocide against his people. Bowers traded gunfire with police and was shot multiple times but survived.

The attack occurred during Sabbath services, when the synagogue's front door was left unlocked, allowing Bowers to enter freely. This vulnerability is common in many houses of worship across the country. Bowers was armed with a rifle and three handguns and targeted a building that housed three separate congregations. The synagogue's staff had not received any prior threats, but security was a concern, and efforts were underway to improve it.

The shooting sparked widespread condemnation and expressions of sympathy from politicians and religious leaders. President Donald Trump ordered flags at federal buildings to be flown at half-staff in respect for the victims and planned to visit Pittsburgh. Thousands gathered for a vigil in the city, with some blaming the slaughter on the nation's political climate. The incident raised alarm in Jewish communities nationwide, prompting authorities to send extra patrols to synagogues and other houses of worship.

The FBI is leading the investigation, which is ongoing. The agency reported an overall increase in hate crimes in 2016, with anti-Semitic incidents accounting for 684 reported cases, more than any other religious minority. The ADL found a significant rise in anti-Semitic incidents last year, with a nearly 60% increase in 2017 compared to the previous year. The organization attributed this surge to a decline in civility and an emboldening of fringe elements in society.

In related news, the FBI reported a 4% increase in hate crimes from 2015 to 2016, with anti-Islamic crimes seeing the largest percentage rise. African Americans were targeted the most in racial hate crimes, followed by incidents motivated by bias against victims based on sexual orientation or gender identity.
